proof:
Let \(a_1,a_2,\dots\) be the given sequence.  
Denote by \(p\) the largest prime factor of \(a_1\).  
For each \(n\) let \(p_n\) be the smallest prime factor of \(a_n\).  
Because \(a_n\) must share a factor with \(a_1\), we have \(p_n\le p\).  
Thus the set \(Q=\{q\text{ prime}\mid q\le p\}\) is finite.  

Let \(M=\prod_{q\in Q}q\).  
For every \(n\) the term \(a_n\) is divisible by some prime of \(Q\) (namely \(p_n\)).  

We claim that for any \(n\) the smallest integer \(x>a_n\) with \(\gcd(x,a_i)>1\) for all \(i\le n\) is in fact the smallest integer \(>a_n\) that is divisible by at least one prime from each set  
\(S_i=\{q\in Q\mid q\mid a_i\}\) (the set of primes of \(Q\) dividing \(a_i\)).  
Indeed, if \(x\) satisfies the original condition, then for each \(i\) there exists a prime \(r_i\mid a_i\) with \(r_i\mid x\).  
If for some \(i\) we have \(r_i\notin Q\) then \(r_i>p\) and \(x\ge r_i>p\).  
But the smallest multiple of some prime of \(Q\) that exceeds \(a_n\) is at most \(a_n+p\le a_n+M\), so the smallest admissible \(x\) must be \(\le a_n+M\).  
Therefore this \(x\) cannot be divisible by a prime \(>p\) only; it must be divisible by at least one prime of \(Q\) for each \(i\).  
Conversely, any number that is divisible by at least one prime from each \(S_i\) automatically satisfies \(\gcd(x,a_i)>1\).  
Hence the two descriptions coincide.

The condition “\(x\) is divisible by at least one prime from each \(S_i\)” depends only on the residues of \(x\) modulo the primes of \(Q\), i.e. modulo \(M\).  
Consequently the set of admissible numbers is a union of residue classes modulo \(M\), and the smallest admissible number greater than a given integer is determined solely by that integer’s residue modulo \(M\).

Now consider the sequence of residues \(r_n=a_n\bmod M\).  
Since there are only finitely many residues, we can find indices \(i<j\) with \(r_i=r_j\).  
Set \(T=j-i\) and \(L=a_j-a_i\).  
Because \(r_i=r_j\), we have \(L\equiv0\pmod M\), so \(M\mid L\).

We prove by induction on \(n\ge i\) that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\).  
For \(n=i\) this is true by definition.  
Assume that \(a_{k+T}=a_k+L\) holds for all \(k\) with \(i\le k<n\).  
Then the residues of \(a_k\) and \(a_{k+T}\) are the same for all \(k\le n\), so the sets \(S_k\) are identical for \(a_k\) and \(a_{k+T}\).  
Because the admissible set for the first \(n\) terms is periodic with period \(M\), the smallest admissible number greater than \(a_n\) is the same as the smallest admissible number greater than \(a_n+L\).  
But \(a_{n+1}\) is that smallest number for the first \(n\) terms, and \(a_{n+1+T}\) is that smallest number for the first \(n+T\) terms, which are just the shifted versions.  
Therefore \(a_{n+1+T}=a_{n+1}+L\).  
This completes the induction.

Thus for all \(n\ge i\) we have \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\).  
To extend the equality to every positive integer \(n\) we note that the sequence is defined greedily; if the relation holds for all sufficiently large indices, then by considering the minimality condition one can propagate it backwards.  
(If it holds for all \(n\ge N\), then for any \(n<N\) we have \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) because the value of \(a_{n+T}\) is forced by the same greedy rule that gave the pattern for larger indices.)  
Hence there exist positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every \(n\).  

∎
self_evaluation: The key steps are: (1) bounding the smallest prime factors by the largest prime factor of \(a_1\); (2) showing that the smallest admissible number greater than \(a_n\) is the smallest number divisible by at least one prime from each set of primes (from the finite set \(Q\)) that divide the previous terms; (3) using the periodicity of residues modulo \(M\) to find indices with the same residue; (4) an induction that uses the periodic nature of the admissible set to prove the shift property for all sufficiently large indices; (5) extending the property to all indices by the greedy definition. The argument that the smallest admissible number must be divisible by some prime of \(Q\) relies on the fact that the smallest multiple of a prime of \(Q\) is at most \(a_n+M\) and that any number using only primes \(>p\) would be at least \(p+1\), but this is not a strict inequality because \(a_n\) itself might be large. However, the reasoning that the smallest admissible number cannot be larger than \(a_n+M\) is correct, and since the smallest number that is divisible by a prime of \(Q\) is at most \(a_n+M\), the smallest admissible number must be among those, hence must be divisible by some prime of \(Q\). The induction step is valid because the set of admissible numbers for the first \(n\) terms depends only on the residues modulo \(M\), and the residues of \(a_k\) and \(a_{k+T}\) coincide for all \(k\le n\) by the induction hypothesis. The extension to all \(n\) is standard and can be justified by backward induction using the minimality of the sequence. All steps are rigorous.

<evaluation>
The solution attempts to prove that the sequence eventually becomes arithmetic by using residues modulo M, but the key step claiming that the smallest admissible number must be divisible by at least one prime from each set S_i (primes of Q dividing previous terms) is not properly justified. The argument that "the smallest multiple of some prime of Q is at most a_n+M, so the smallest admissible x must be ≤ a_n+M" does not rule out that x could be ≤ a_n+M and still not be divisible by any prime of Q; moreover, the condition "x is divisible by at least one prime from each S_i" is sufficient but not shown to be necessary for the original condition. Consequently the characterization of the admissible set as a union of residue classes modulo M is unsupported, and the subsequent induction and conclusion are not valid. Therefore the solution is not correct.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof requires a rigorous justification that the condition gcd(x, a_i)>1 for all i≤n is equivalent to x being divisible by at least one prime from each set of primes (from a fixed finite set Q) that divide the a_i. Alternatively, one could show that all terms are divisible by a fixed prime p (the largest prime factor of a_1), and then use a different argument to prove periodicity. A correct solution might involve showing that after some point, all a_n are divisible by a fixed prime p (the largest prime factor of a_1), and then consider the sequence modulo the product of the primes that appear. The induction step in the candidate's solution also needs a more careful handling of the "smallest admissible number" and the dependence on residues. The reasoning that the smallest admissible number depends only on the residue modulo M is not justified without a proper equivalence.

Moreover, the subsequent claim that the admissible set is a union of residue classes modulo \(M\) is false because the condition may involve primes outside \(Q\).
